reasoning: Adam Peaty has held the men's 50m breaststroke long course world record (25.95) since July 2017, and the record has survived numerous World Championships. The closest active competitor is Qin Haiyang, whose personal best is 26.20—a massive 0.25 seconds away from Peaty's mark, which is a very significant gap in a 50m sprint. Since there are no major international long-course championships set before mid-June, the record is nearly certain to survive the first two resolution dates (limited only by the small chance of an unannounced record at a national trial). The main test for the record will be the 2025 World Aquatics Championships in Singapore, running from July 11 to August 3, 2025. While there is a non-trivial chance that Qin Haiyang or another swimmer could drop a historic swim there, the 8-year durability of this record and the sheer size of the gap to the rest of the field make it highly probable that Peaty will retain the record through the summer and the rest of 2025. Even if Peaty breaks his own record, the question resolves YES because he would still hold it. Thus, probabilities remain quite high, slowly decaying after the World Championships.
